comments:
Summary:
This paper proposes a novel probabilistic model for document-level content structure that represents a document’s topic ordering as a permutation drawn from a Generalized Mallows Model (GMM). The model also samples per-document topic frequency and per-topic language models, and enforces topic contiguity by construction. Inference is performed via collapsed Gibbs sampling with slice sampling for GMM dispersion parameters; practical computation is sped up with memoization. The model is evaluated on three discourse-level tasks — cross-document alignment, segmentation, and ordering — on multiple datasets (Wikipedia, reviews, multilingual), and compared to several strong baselines (HTMM, BayesSeg, HMM-based content models). The paper reports substantial empirical improvements and provides analyses of two constrained variants (Constrained, Uniform) to isolate the contribution of the permutation prior.

Strengths:
- Clear, well-motivated modeling contribution: encoding global ordering constraints via permutations (GMM) is a principled and compact way to capture cross-document ordering regularities and topic contiguity.
- The use of the GMM (including its inversion representation and conjugate prior) is technically apt: it offers linear parameter scaling and integrates smoothly in a hierarchical Bayesian framework.
- Multiple realistic tasks are considered (alignment, segmentation, ordering), showing the model’s versatility; empirical gains appear substantial across datasets and settings.
- Good ablation/variant analysis (Constrained and Uniform variants) that helps illuminate when permutation bias helps and when too much flexibility harms (e.g., very formulaic domains).
- Practical engineering details (memoized caching, efficient computation of document likelihoods) are careful and important for feasibility.
- Authors acknowledge limitations (e.g., dialogue domains where topics recur) and sketch sensible extensions (mixtures of GMMs, hyperpriors, combining with local coherence models).
- Code and data are made available, aiding reproducibility.

Weaknesses / Issues / Suggestions:
- Evaluation relies largely on author-provided section headings as gold annotations, which the authors call “noisy.” While a manual cleaned set is used for one corpus (CitiesEn), broader manual gold standards or human judgments (especially for ordering quality) would strengthen the claims. A human evaluation of ordering quality or coherence would help validate Kendall’s τ correlations.
- The choice of the number of topics K is user-specified. Although the authors note that the model can use fewer than K topics per document, the paper lacks an empirical study of sensitivity to K or a principled method (e.g., nonparametric extension) to choose it. Some experiments exploring influence of K and robustness would be useful.
- Inference: the paper reports using the 10,000th iteration sample from five chains. More discussion or diagnostics about sampler convergence, mixing, and computational cost (wall-clock times, scalability with corpus size and K) would help assess practical applicability to larger corpora. Also, relying on a single sample per chain may underrepresent posterior uncertainty; reporting averaged predictions or consistency across samples would be helpful.
- Computational cost of slice sampling for each ρj and repeated resampling across many documents could be substantial; more empirical characterization of training time and memory usage would assist practitioners.
- The paper abstains from comparing to some discriminative or reranking approaches for ordering that have shown competitive performance in certain settings; though the authors explain why direct comparisons are difficult, a brief empirical comparison on a common subtask or discussion of how the models could be combined (e.g., using permutation priors as features) would be valuable.
- The Uniform variant is noted as unsuitable for ordering; this is reasonable but worth clarifying earlier when variants are introduced.

Minor / Presentation:
- Overall exposition is clear and thorough. A few algorithmic steps (e.g., explicit pseudocode for the collapsed sampler loop, or more detail on slice sampling settings) would help reproducibility. Some extra runtime/memory numbers would be helpful to the reader.

Recommendation / Verdict:
This is a solid, well-written, and technically principled paper that makes a clear and original contribution to document-level content modeling. It advances modeling by introducing a tractable global permutation prior for topic orderings, integrates it into a hierarchical Bayesian framework, and demonstrates practical utility across several important discourse tasks. Remaining concerns are mainly empirical/practical (convergence diagnostics, sensitivity to K, reliance on noisy headings), but these do not undermine the novelty or soundness of the approach. I recommend acceptance / broad dissemination.
